title: Humility is Glorious
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 8-18-24 -AM- Humility is Glorious - 1 Peter 5_1-7 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Glory of Humility
subpoints: We don't normally think of humility as glorious — yet the Bible clearly teaches that the glory of a person is found in humility
God exalts the humble and opposes the proud
Two tests of humility in this passage: submission to authority and trust in God rather than anxiety

outline.title: Test One: Submission to Authority and Each Other
subpoints: Verse 5 continues the preceding section (vv. 1-4) on elders and the flock with the word "likewise"
"Younger people" addressed because they are the most likely not to submit to authority — an implicit call to all
Lack of submission is a sign of immaturity; independence from authority is not a Christian virtue
Those who pride themselves on not submitting to authority will experience God's resistance and the withdrawal of his grace
Christians should only refuse to submit when biblically required — and even then with great sorrow, not rejoicing

outline.title: Defining Humility
subpoints: Humility: an attitude which puts others first; thinking of the desires, needs, and ideas of others as more worthy of attention than one's own
Philippians 2:3-9: let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in humility let each esteem others better than himself — the mind of Christ
Humility is not self-deprecation (which often seeks validation) — if you are good at something, denying it is not humility
Proverbs 27:2: let another praise you and not your own lips
Humility is not thinking poorly of yourself — it is not thinking about yourself at all
Humility is total and ultimate dependence on God, like a child's total dependence on parents

outline.title: The Warrant for Humility: God's Response to Pride and Humility
subpoints: Proverbs 3:34 (quoted in v. 5): God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble
The proud trust in himself instead of God — God trains the proud by allowing them to live a life of self-reliance, which does not work
The humble trusts in God — and since God delights in being trusted, he gives the humble more grace
The proud seeks glory for himself; the humble gives glory to God — Psalm 115:1
1 Corinthians 4:7: what do you have that you did not receive? If you received it, why do you boast as if you had not received it?

outline.title: Test Two: Trust in God Rather Than Anxiety
subpoints: Verse 6: humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time
Humility before God means bowing to his wisdom, accepting the twists and turns of his providence
Anxiety is the manifestation of a proud heart — it is self-centered and opposed to humility
Verse 7: cast all your anxieties on him, for he cares for you — the casting is how we humble ourselves
Proof that God will care for us: Philippians 2:9 — because Christ humbled himself, God highly exalted him; the resurrection is the proof that God exalts the humble
Ephesians 3:20-21: God is able to do exceedingly abundantly above all that we ask or think

summary: Preaching from 1 Peter 5:1-7, Pastor Lyro titles the sermon "Humility is Glorious" to challenge the common assumption that humility is something meek and unremarkable. He structures the sermon around two tests of humility drawn from the passage: submission to authority, and trust in God rather than anxiety. On the first test, he examines Peter's exhortation to younger people to submit to elders and to all to submit to one another — arguing that the younger are addressed because they are the most likely to resist authority, and that pride in independence is a sign of spiritual immaturity. He defines humility carefully, distinguishing it from self-deprecation (which actually seeks validation) and noting that true humility is not thinking poorly of yourself but ceasing to think about yourself at all. He grounds humility in Proverbs 3:34 — God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble — and shows from 1 Corinthians 4:7 that all we have was received as a gift. On the second test, he links anxiety directly to pride: anxiety is self-centered, constantly focused on one's own needs, and therefore antithetical to the other-centeredness of humility. The path of humility is casting all anxieties on God, who cares for us — the guarantee of which is the resurrection of Christ, the supreme example of God exalting the one who humbled himself.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 1 Peter 5:1-7

If you're able, would you remain standing for a moment longer as we read from God's Word in 1 Peter chapter 5, verses 1 through 7. This is the Word of our Lord.

The elders who are among you I exhort, I who am a fellow elder and a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that will be revealed: shepherd the flock of God which is among you, serving as overseers, not by compulsion but willingly, not for dishonest gain but eagerly; nor as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock. And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that does not fade away.

Likewise, you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders. Yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. Therefore humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time, casting all your care upon him, for he cares for you.

## Introduction: Humility is Glorious

If you look at the bulletin, you are going to see that I titled this sermon, Humility is Glorious. I did that because we don't think that way about humility often — as being a glorious thing. Yet the Bible clearly teaches that the glory of a person is in his or her humility. Even here in our passage, Peter teaches that God exalts the humble and he also opposes the proud.

I hope you will stay for the afternoon service at one thirty, where we are going to consider what pride is. This morning I want us to focus on humility. There is glory in humility because it is a characteristic of Christ, our Lord and Savior.

In our passage today, the Holy Spirit through the Apostle Peter exhorts us to pursue humility. In doing that, he gives us two tests of humility. That is what we are going to structure our sermon around. The first one is represented by the question: do you submit to authority and to each other? The second one is: do you trust in God, or are you an anxious person? These are two questions by which I hope you will check whether you are walking in humility.

## Test One: Submission to Authority and Each Other

The first thing we see in verse 5 is that humility is displayed in submission to authority and each other. Peter says, likewise, you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders. Yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility.

We see right off the bat that Peter is continuing what he had begun in verses 1-4 — we know that because of the word "likewise" at the beginning. Peter has been teaching us how elders have to relate to the flock; verse 5 gives us insights on how the flock should relate to the elders.

Peter says, likewise, you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders. Some suggest that all Peter is saying here is that younger people should be respectful to older people. But the context requires that we understand the word "elders" in its technical sense, because Peter used it that way in verses 1-4 — talking about the elders, the shepherds of the church.

Now the question becomes: why does Peter address the younger people specifically? Well, it is suggested that Peter addresses younger people because they are the most likely not to submit to the authority of elders in the church. That's why he addresses them. Peter is saying that in the same way the elders are to spend themselves in overseeing the flock in humility, the flock is to submit to them in humility. And even though he says "younger people," he's making the point that lack of submission is a sign of immaturity.

So in Peter's explicit statement for the younger people to submit to the elders in humility, there is an implicit exhortation for all of the church: if the most immature group is to submit to the elders in humility, so are the most mature and everyone in between.

You know, some people pride themselves on being independent. I don't follow anybody. I'm my own person. I don't submit to anybody. I am the greatest. But interestingly, Peter says that attitude is actually immaturity. Only an immature person would think that that is a good thing. And that attitude — as Peter quotes Proverbs 3:34 — leads to God resisting you. So if you're a Christian who prides yourself in your independence, in not submitting to authority, you're just going to reap what you're sowing: God's resistance, God's refusing to hear you, God putting you aside, and you experiencing the withdrawing of his grace from you.

Christians who are walking in humility will submit to authority and will only refuse to submit when biblically necessary. And even then with great sorrow. Christians should never rejoice in rebellion against authority. It should always be sorrowful, hurtful, something we would rather not do when we have to resist the authorities in our lives.

And not only are we to submit to the elders of the church, but we are also to submit to each other. Verse 5 again: yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility. Not just the young, not just the old, not just the officers — all are here commanded to put on humility in the way we relate to one another.

## Defining Humility

We've been talking about humility but we haven't defined it yet. One commentator gives a helpful definition: the term humility speaks of an attitude which puts others first, which thinks of the desires, needs, and ideas of others as more worthy of attention than one's own. Humility listens to God's advice even when it is contrary to what one wants to do.

Philippians 2:3-4 defines humility well and gives Christ's incarnation and death as the ultimate example. Paul says, let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit — those two words are synonymous with pride — but in humility, let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests but also for the interests of others. And then he says, let this mind which was in Christ Jesus be also in you. Who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross.

How do I do this? Submit to God and what God wants, submit to the work of the spiritual life — and that's how you're going to be able to consider others better than yourself.

Now, humility is not self-deprecation. Self-deprecation is often not humility but pride, because you want somebody to say, oh no, that's not true, don't think so little of yourself. Try this: next time somebody makes a self-deprecating comment, say, I agree. See what reaction you get. Because usually those self-deprecating comments are putting feelings out there to get people to pet your ego. So self-deprecation is not humility. If you're good at something, denying it doesn't make you humble. Humility includes acknowledging the gift that God has given you and using it in the service of others.

Humility is really not thinking about yourself as your primary concern. How much of your time do you spend thinking about yourself? That's the test of humility as well. Proverbs 27 says, let another man praise you and not your own mouth, a stranger and not your own lips.

And in the end, humility is total and ultimate dependence on another — namely, on God through Jesus Christ. Because it is all of grace. Where is Abigail? She's right there. Think of how totally dependent Abigail is. She cannot help you in the house. She cannot clean or cook. She is totally dependent on her parents. And God says, that's your example. Totally dependent on Christ. That's what humility is.

Humility is a characteristic of heavenly living. Every Christian, Paul says in Colossians 3:12, is called to humility. In Titus 3:1-2, Paul says, remind them to be subject to rulers and authorities, to obey, to be ready for every good work, to speak evil of no one, to be peaceable, gentle, showing all humility to all men. Brothers and sisters, we owe humility to all people. There is not one person to whom we don't owe humility. Faith and humility go hand in hand. Matthew 18:4 — whoever humbles himself as this little child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.

## The Warrant: God Resists the Proud, Gives Grace to the Humble

Peter grounds his teaching on Scripture by quoting Proverbs 3:34: God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. God actively resists the proud, the arrogant. He does that because the proud trust in themselves instead of God. So God trains the proud by allowing them to live a life of self-reliance — because that life doesn't work. We need people. We need God.

The humble, though, trusts in God. And since God delights in being trusted, he gives the humble more grace for life. As we grow in humility, God gives us more humility — the ability to be humble. The proud seeks glory for himself. The humble gives glory to God. Psalm 115:1 is the song of the humble: not unto us, Lord, not unto us, but to your name give glory, because of your mercy, because of your truth.

The humble recognizes that all he has was undeservedly given to him by God. In 1 Corinthians 4:7, Paul says, for who makes you differ from one another? And what do you have that you did not receive? Now if you did indeed receive it, why do you boast as if you had not received it? Everything is God's. Everything we have is given to us. So in what do we boast?

## Test Two: Trust in God Rather Than Anxiety

And as we finish this morning, let me turn to the second test of humility, in verses 6 and 7. Therefore humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time, casting all your care upon him, for he cares for you.

Humility before God involves bowing down to God's wisdom, accepting and trusting the twists and turns of his providence, and entrusting all our concerns to him. God gave us a chance to practice that recently — our flight home was delayed and diverted, we landed at 12:30 AM, we waited on the tarmac for two and a half hours, and we got home at four in the morning. All the proud planning — we have all these things built in, we're going to be home by 9:30, look how well we're keeping the Sabbath — gone. And that's a good thing. Because humility involves bowing down to God's wisdom, accepting and trusting the twists and turns of his providence.

Notice the promise of verse 6: humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time. Brothers, God will exalt the humble. Sisters, God will exalt the humble. If not in this life, in the life to come. And what is the proof of that? The resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ. Philippians 2:9 — therefore, because Christ humbled himself and became obedient to death, God also highly exalted him and gave him the name which is above every name. That at the name of Jesus every knee should bow — of those in heaven, and of those on earth — and that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father. The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the proof that God exalts the humble.

And Peter tells us that true humility is attained by casting all our anxieties on the one who truly cares for us. Verse 7, casting all your care upon him, for he cares for you. If you notice, the first word of verse 7 in most translations will have an "-ing" ending: casting. That's explaining how we humble ourselves. We humble ourselves by casting all these things upon the Lord Jesus Christ.

Someone might think, well, if I put others first and focus on their interests, who will take care of me? Peter's answer is: God will. And he can do a better job of it than we can. Ephesians 3:20-21: now to him who is able to do exceedingly abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that works in us, to him be glory in the church by Christ Jesus to all generations, forever and ever. God gives us all the grace we need to be humble.

And this is very important: anxiety is the manifestation of a proud heart. Anxiety by nature is proud and self-centered. Humility is others-oriented. Casting all your anxieties on God is the path of humility, freeing you from constant concern with yourself and enabling you truly to be concerned for the needs of others.

An anxious person is in a self-made, self-imposed prison that keeps him from serving others and finding God. There is nobody else making you anxious — besides yourself. So it's a prison you made, but the door is unlocked. You just have to push through and get out.

How do you know that God cares for you? He would not spare his own Son, but delivered him up for us all. How shall he not with him also freely give us all things? He gave us Jesus. And Jesus is alive, ruling, gifting his church, that we all may grow into his image.

Let us pray. Father in heaven, we thank you that you are a good God. We thank you that you are a God who speaks. Father, so many distractions in our midst today. We pray that you would bring our thoughts together, that we might be able to meditate upon the words that were said here. Humble us in your sight. We pray that we will be able to cast all our anxieties upon you, because we know that you care for us. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
